From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Largest social unit in traditional Māori culture

This article is about the Māori social unit. For other uses, see [Iwi (disambiguation)](/wiki/Iwi_(disambiguation) "Iwi (disambiguation)").

**Iwi** (Māori pronunciation: [[ˈiwi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori")) are the largest social units in New Zealand [Māori society](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture "Māori culture"). In [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"), *iwi* roughly means 'people' or 'nation',[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallara1998Back_cover-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) and is often translated as "[tribe](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe")".[[3]](#cite_note-3) The word is both singular and [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural") in the Māori language, and is typically pluralised as such in English.

Iwi groups trace their ancestry to the original [Polynesian](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") migrants who, according to tradition, arrived from [Hawaiki](/wiki/Hawaiki "Hawaiki"). Some iwi cluster into larger groupings that are based on [whakapapa](/wiki/Whakapapa "Whakapapa") (genealogical tradition) and known as [waka](/wiki/Waka_(canoe) "Waka (canoe)") (literally '[canoes](/wiki/Canoe "Canoe")', with reference to the [original migration voyages](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_migration_canoes "Māori migration canoes")). These super-groupings are generally symbolic rather than logistical. In pre-European times, most Māori were allied to relatively small groups in the form of *[hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū")* ('sub-tribes')[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallara199817-4) and [whānau](/wiki/Wh%C4%81nau "Whānau") ('family').[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallara1998164-5) Each iwi contains a number of hapū; among the hapū of the [Ngāti Whātua](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Wh%C4%81tua "Ngāti Whātua") iwi, for example, are [Te Uri-o-Hau](/wiki/Te_Uri-o-Hau "Te Uri-o-Hau"), [Te Roroa](/wiki/Te_Roroa "Te Roroa"), [Te Taoū](/wiki/Te_Tao%C5%AB "Te Taoū"), and [Ngāti Whātua-o-Ōrākei](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Wh%C4%81tua-o-%C5%8Cr%C4%81kei "Ngāti Whātua-o-Ōrākei"). Māori use the word *[rohe](/wiki/Rohe "Rohe")* for the territory or boundaries of iwi.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In modern-day New Zealand, iwi can exercise significant [political power](/wiki/Political_power "Political power") in the management of land and other assets. For example, the 1997 [Treaty of Waitangi settlement](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi_settlement "Treaty of Waitangi settlement") between the New Zealand Government and [Ngāi Tahu](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_Tahu "Ngāi Tahu") compensated iwi for various losses of the rights guaranteed under the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") of 1840. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit) the tribe has collective [assets under management](/wiki/Assets_under_management "Assets under management") of $1.85 billion.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Iwi affairs can have a real impact on [New Zealand politics](/wiki/Politics_of_New_Zealand "Politics of New Zealand") and society. A 2004 attempt by some iwi to test in court their ownership of the seabed and foreshore areas polarised public opinion (see [New Zealand foreshore and seabed controversy](/wiki/New_Zealand_foreshore_and_seabed_controversy "New Zealand foreshore and seabed controversy")).

## Naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Naming")]

In Māori and many other [Polynesian languages](/wiki/Polynesian_languages "Polynesian languages"), *iwi* literally means 'bone',[[8]](#cite_note-8) derived from [Proto-Oceanic](/wiki/Proto-Oceanic_language "Proto-Oceanic language") \**suRi*₁, meaning 'thorn, splinter, fish bone'.[[9]](#cite_note-ACD-9) Māori may refer to returning home after travelling or living elsewhere as "going back to the bones" — literally to the burial areas of [ancestors](/wiki/Ancestor "Ancestor"). Māori author [Keri Hulme](/wiki/Keri_Hulme "Keri Hulme")'s novel *[The Bone People](/wiki/The_Bone_People "The Bone People")* (1985) has a title linked directly to this dual meaning of bone and "tribal people".

Many iwi names begin with *Ngāti* or with *Ngāi* (from *ngā āti* and *ngā ai* respectively, both meaning roughly 'the offspring of'). *Ngāti* has become a productive [morpheme](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme") in [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English") to refer to groups of people: examples are Ngāti Pākehā ([Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā") as a group), [Ngāti Pōneke](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_P%C5%8Dneke "Ngāti Pōneke") (Māori who have migrated to the [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington") region), and [Ngāti Rānana](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_R%C4%81nana "Ngāti Rānana") (Māori living in London). Ngāti Tūmatauenga ("Tribe of [Tūmatauenga](/wiki/T%C5%ABmatauenga "Tūmatauenga")", the god of war) is the official Māori-language name of the [New Zealand Army](/wiki/New_Zealand_Army "New Zealand Army"). [Hawaiian](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") navigator [Nainoa Thompson](/wiki/Nainoa_Thompson "Nainoa Thompson") and his crew upon the [Hōkūleʻa](/wiki/H%C5%8Dk%C5%ABle%CA%BBa "Hōkūleʻa") canoe were inducted among the [Te Tai Tokerau Māori](/wiki/Te_Tai_Tokerau_M%C4%81ori "Te Tai Tokerau Māori") by [James Henare](/wiki/James_Henare "James Henare") as the iwi of *Ngāti Ruawāhia* (“Tribe of the [Arcturus](/wiki/Arcturus "Arcturus")”) after their successful voyage from [Rarotonga](/wiki/Rarotonga "Rarotonga") to Waitangi in 1985, the admission of Ngāti Ruawāhia was formalised in 2018.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In the [southern dialect](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language#South_Island_dialects "Māori language") of Māori, Ngāti and Ngāi become *Kāti* and *Kāi*, terms found in such iwi names as [Kāti Māmoe](/wiki/K%C4%81ti_M%C4%81moe "Kāti Māmoe") and [Kāi Tahu](/wiki/K%C4%81i_Tahu "Kāi Tahu") (also known as Ngāi Tahu).

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure")]

Each iwi has a generally recognised territory (*[rohe](/wiki/Rohe "Rohe")*), but many of these overlap, sometimes completely.[[11]](#cite_note-11) This has added a layer of complication to the long-running discussions and court cases about how to resolve historical Treaty claims. The length of coastline emerged as one factor in the final (2004) legislation to allocate fishing-rights in settlement of claims relating to commercial fisheries.

### Self-determination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Self-determination")]

Iwi can become a prospective vehicle for ideas and ideals of [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") and/or *[tino rangatiratanga](/wiki/Tino_rangatiratanga "Tino rangatiratanga")*. Thus does [Te Pāti Māori](/wiki/Te_P%C4%81ti_M%C4%81ori "Te Pāti Māori") mention in the preamble of its constitution "the dreams and aspirations of [tangata whenua](/wiki/Tangata_whenua "Tangata whenua") to achieve self-determination for whānau, hapū and iwi within their own land".[[12]](#cite_note-12) Some [Tūhoe](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_T%C5%ABhoe "Ngāi Tūhoe") envisage self-determination in specifically iwi-oriented terms.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Iwi identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Iwi identity")]

Increasing urbanisation of Māori has led to a situation where a significant percentage do not identify with any particular iwi. The following extract from a 2000 [High Court of New Zealand](/wiki/High_Court_of_New_Zealand "High Court of New Zealand") judgment discussing the process of settling fishing rights illustrates some of the issues:

> ... 81 per cent of Maori now live in urban areas, at least one-third live outside their tribal influence, more than one-quarter do not know their iwi or for some reason do not choose to affiliate with it, at least 70 per cent live outside the traditional tribal territory and these will have difficulties, which in many cases will be severe, in both relating to their tribal heritage and in accessing benefits from the settlement. It is also said that many Maori reject tribal affiliation because of a working-class unemployed attitude, defiance and frustration. Related but less important factors, are that a hapu may belong to more than one iwi, a particular hapu may have belonged to different iwi at different times, the tension caused by the social and economic power moving from the iwi down rather than from the hapu up, and the fact that many iwi do not recognise spouses and adoptees who do not have kinship links.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In the 2006 census, 16 per cent of the 643,977 people who claimed Māori ancestry did not know their iwi. Another 11 per cent did not state their iwi, or stated only a general geographic region, or merely gave a *[waka](/wiki/Waka_(canoe) "Waka (canoe)")* name.[[15]](#cite_note-iwi_Census-15) Initiatives like the Iwi Helpline are trying to make it easier for people to identify their iwi,[[16]](#cite_note-iwihelpline-16) and the proportion who "don't know" dropped relative to previous censuses.[[15]](#cite_note-iwi_Census-15)

### Pan-tribalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pan-tribalism")]

Some established pan-tribal organisations may[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] exert influence across iwi divisions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Rātana](/wiki/R%C4%81tana "Rātana") Church, for example, operates across iwi divisions, and the [Māori King Movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_King_Movement "Māori King Movement"), though principally congregated around [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato")/[Tainui](/wiki/Tainui "Tainui"), aims to transcend some iwi functions in a wider grouping.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Major iwi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Major iwi")]

Main article: [List of iwi](/wiki/List_of_iwi "List of iwi")

### Largest iwi by population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Largest iwi by population")]

1. [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi") – 165,201 (in 2018) – based in the [Northland Region](/wiki/Northland_Region "Northland Region")
2. [Ngāti Porou](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Porou "Ngāti Porou") – 92,349 (in 2018) – based in [Gisborne Region](/wiki/Gisborne,_New_Zealand "Gisborne, New Zealand") and [East Cape](/wiki/East_Cape "East Cape")
3. [Waikato Tainui](/wiki/Waikato_Tainui "Waikato Tainui") – 84,030 (in 2018)[[17]](#cite_note-Demographics-17) – based in the [Waikato Region](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato")
4. [Ngāti Kahungunu](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Kahungunu "Ngāti Kahungunu") – 82,239 (in 2018) based on the East Coast of the North Island.
5. [Ngāi Tahu/ Kāi Tahu](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_Tahu "Ngāi Tahu") – 74,082[[17]](#cite_note-Demographics-17)(in 2018) based in the South Island.
6. [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") – 60,719 (in 2018) – based in the [Bay of Plenty Region](/wiki/North_Island "North Island")
7. [Ngāti Tūwharetoa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_T%C5%ABwharetoa "Ngāti Tūwharetoa") – 47,930 (in 2018) – based in the central [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island").
8. [Ngāi Tūhoe](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_T%C5%ABhoe "Ngāi Tūhoe") – 46,479 (in 2018)[[17]](#cite_note-Demographics-17) – based in [Te Urewera](/wiki/Te_Urewera "Te Urewera") and [Whakatāne](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne "Whakatāne")
9. [Ngāti Maniapoto](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Maniapoto "Ngāti Maniapoto") – 45,719 (in 2018) – based in [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") and [Waitomo](/wiki/Waitomo "Waitomo")
10. [Ngāti Raukawa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Raukawa "Ngāti Raukawa") – 31,029 (in 2023) – based in [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato"), [Manawatū–Whanganui](/wiki/Manawat%C5%AB%E2%80%93Whanganui "Manawatū–Whanganui"), and [Greater Wellington](/wiki/Greater_Wellington "Greater Wellington").

### Other iwi by population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other iwi by population")]

1. No affiliation – 110,928 (in 2013) – includes New-Zealand-based Māori with no iwi affiliation
2. Te Hiku, or [Muriwhenua](/wiki/Muriwhenua "Muriwhenua") – 33,711 (in 2013) – group of iwi and *hapū* in the [Northland region](/wiki/Northland_region "Northland region")
3. [Te Āti Awa](/wiki/Te_%C4%80ti_Awa "Te Āti Awa") – 23,094 (in 2013) – group of iwi and *hapū* in [Taranaki](/wiki/Taranaki "Taranaki") and [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington")
4. [Hauraki Māori](/wiki/Hauraki_M%C4%81ori "Hauraki Māori") – 14,313 (in 2013) – group of iwi and *hapū* at or around the [Hauraki Gulf](/wiki/Hauraki_Gulf "Hauraki Gulf")

### Other notable iwi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other notable iwi")]

* [Ngāti Toa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Toa "Ngāti Toa") (based in [Porirua](/wiki/Porirua "Porirua"), having migrated from [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") in the 1820s under the leadership of [Te Rauparaha](/wiki/Te_Rauparaha "Te Rauparaha"))
* [Ngāti Tama](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Tama "Ngāti Tama") (based in [Taranaki](/wiki/Taranaki "Taranaki"), [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands"), [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington") and [Te Tau Ihu](/wiki/Te_Tau_Ihu_M%C4%81ori "Te Tau Ihu Māori"))
* [Ngāti Ruanui](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Ruanui "Ngāti Ruanui") (based in the Taranaki region)
* [Ngāruahine](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ruahine "Ngāruahine") (based in South Taranaki)
* [Te Āti Awa](/wiki/Te_%C4%80ti_Awa "Te Āti Awa") – Taranaki and [Lower Hutt](/wiki/Lower_Hutt "Lower Hutt")
* [Ngāti Hikairo](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Hikairo "Ngāti Hikairo") -rangatiratanga in Kāwhia, Ōpārau and Waipā in the [King Country](/wiki/King_Country "King Country"))
* [Whakatōhea](/wiki/Whakat%C5%8Dhea "Whakatōhea") (based in the [Ōpōtiki](/wiki/%C5%8Cp%C5%8Dtiki "Ōpōtiki") district)
* [Ngāti Whātua](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Wh%C4%81tua "Ngāti Whātua") (based in and north of [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland") – notably [Bastion Point](/wiki/Bastion_Point "Bastion Point") in [Ōrākei](/wiki/%C5%8Cr%C4%81kei "Ōrākei"))

## Iwi radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Iwi&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Iwi radio")]

Main article: [Te Whakaruruhau o Ngā Reo Irirangi Māori](/wiki/Te_Whakaruruhau_o_Ng%C4%81_Reo_Irirangi_M%C4%81ori "Te Whakaruruhau o Ngā Reo Irirangi Māori")

Many iwi operate or are affiliated with media organisations. Most of these belong to [Te Whakaruruhau o Ngā Reo Irirangi Māori](/wiki/Te_Whakaruruhau_o_Ng%C4%81_Reo_Irirangi_M%C4%81ori "Te Whakaruruhau o Ngā Reo Irirangi Māori") (the National Māori Radio Network), a group of radio stations which receive contestable Government funding from [Te Māngai Pāho](/wiki/Te_M%C4%81ngai_P%C4%81ho "Te Māngai Pāho") (the Māori Broadcast Funding Agency) to operate on behalf of iwi and hapū. Under their funding agreement, the stations must produce programmes in the local Māori language and actively promote local Māori culture.[[18]](#cite_note-maorimedia-18)

A two-year [Massey University](/wiki/Massey_University "Massey University") survey of 30,000 people published in 2003 indicated 50 per cent of Māori in National Māori Radio Network broadcast areas listened to an iwi station.[[19]](#cite_note-hiddensuccess-19) An [Auckland University of Technology](/wiki/Auckland_University_of_Technology "Auckland University of Technology") study in 2009 suggested the audience of iwi radio stations would increase as the growing New Zealand Māori population tried to keep a connection to their culture, family history, spirituality, community, language and iwi.[[20]](#cite_note-diversityreportage-20)

The [Victoria University of Wellington](/wiki/Victoria_University_of_Wellington "Victoria University of Wellington") Te Reo Māori Society campaigned for Māori radio, helping to set up Te Reo o Poneke, the first Māori-owned radio operation, using airtime on Wellington [student-radio](/wiki/Campus_radio "Campus radio") station [Radio Active](/wiki/Radio_Active_(New_Zealand) "Radio Active (New Zealand)") in 1983.[[21]](#cite_note-walkerfirstiwi-21) Twenty-one iwi radio stations were set up between 1989 and 1994, receiving Government funding in accordance with a Treaty of Waitangi claim.[[22]](#cite_note-cherrylsmith-22) This group of radio stations formed various networks, becoming Te Whakaruruhau o Ngā Reo Irirangi Māori.[[23]](#cite_note-avcgroupeu-23)
